title: A Salary Negotiation
summary: During a performance review, an employee named Sarah negotiates a salary increase with her manager David, navigating competing priorities, company budget constraints, and her own market value.

body.speaker: Manager
body.text: Thanks for coming in, Sarah. As you know, we're doing annual reviews this week, and I wanted to talk through your performance over the past year before we get to the compensation side of things.

body.speaker: Employee
body.text: I appreciate that. I've genuinely enjoyed the year and I think there's been a lot of strong work to reflect on. But I'll admit, the compensation conversation is the one I've been most preparing for.

body.speaker: Manager
body.text: Fair enough. Let me start by saying your performance has been excellent — the Henderson account turnaround alone was a significant contribution. The team has noticed your leadership in that project.

body.speaker: Employee
body.text: Thank you. That project was genuinely challenging. I was managing three junior staff, coordinating with external consultants, and we brought the client back from the brink of cancelling the contract. I think that deserves to be reflected in my package.

body.speaker: Manager
body.text: I completely agree with that in principle. The challenge, as I'm sure you're aware, is that we're operating in a tighter budget environment this year than last. Headcount costs have gone up significantly across the business.

body.speaker: Employee
body.text: I understand the budget pressure, and I don't want to be unreasonable. But I've also done some research into market rates for my role at this level, and I'm currently sitting below the median for comparable positions in this sector.

body.speaker: Manager
body.text: What figure are you working with, if you don't mind me asking?

body.speaker: Employee
body.text: The data I've seen puts the median at roughly fifteen percent above my current base. I'm not asking for fifteen percent — I recognise we're in a constrained environment. But I think a meaningful increase is warranted.

body.speaker: Manager
body.text: I appreciate you coming prepared with data rather than just a number. What would 'meaningful' look like to you?

body.speaker: Employee
body.text: I was thinking in the region of ten percent. That would still leave me below the market median, but it would acknowledge the contributions I've made and close some of the gap.

body.speaker: Manager
body.text: Ten percent is at the higher end of what I have discretion over, honestly. What I can authorise unilaterally is closer to six or seven. Anything beyond that needs sign-off from the director, which adds time and isn't guaranteed.

body.speaker: Employee
body.text: I appreciate the transparency. Can I ask — if we were to go through the director, what would strengthen the case? Is it purely a budget question, or is there a performance threshold that would make it easier to justify?

body.speaker: Manager
body.text: Honestly, it's both. The budget argument is real, but the more compelling case would be tied to scope — if we could frame it as a role expansion rather than just a salary adjustment, that's an easier conversation to have with the director.

body.speaker: Employee
body.text: That's actually interesting to hear. I've been informally taking on some responsibilities that weren't in my original job description — particularly around client onboarding and the new CRM implementation. Could those be formalised?

body.speaker: Manager
body.text: That's a strong angle. If we document those responsibilities and put together a revised job description, I think there's a genuinely good case for a title change as well, which would support the salary conversation at the same time.

body.speaker: Employee
body.text: I'm open to that. I'd want to make sure the title change comes with the salary increase rather than being offered instead of it — I've heard of that happening before.

body.speaker: Manager
body.text: That's a fair concern and I want to be straight with you: I wouldn't put you in that position. My intention would be to pursue both together. Let me take this to the director with a full proposal. Can I come back to you by the end of next week?

body.speaker: Employee
body.text: That works. And thank you for being open to this conversation — I think it's going in a direction that could work well for both sides.

annotations.meaningVi: đánh giá hàng năm — cuộc họp đánh giá hiệu suất và mức lương định kỳ
annotations.exampleEn: She prepared a portfolio of achievements before her annual review.

annotations.meaningVi: trên bờ vực của, gần như đã xảy ra
annotations.exampleEn: The negotiations pulled the company back from the brink of bankruptcy.

annotations.meaningVi: gói thù lao, tổng lợi ích tài chính nhận được từ công việc
annotations.exampleEn: The compensation package included salary, bonuses, and stock options.

annotations.meaningVi: môi trường ngân sách thắt chặt, hạn chế chi tiêu hơn
annotations.exampleEn: In a tighter budget environment, salary increases are harder to justify.

annotations.meaningVi: chi phí nhân sự, tổng chi phí cho số lượng nhân viên
annotations.exampleEn: Rising headcount costs forced the company to freeze new hiring.

annotations.meaningVi: mức lương thị trường — mức lương phổ biến cho vai trò tương đương
annotations.exampleEn: She benchmarked her salary against market rates before entering negotiations.

annotations.meaningVi: mức trung bình thị trường, điểm giữa của phân phối lương
annotations.exampleEn: Her current salary was well below the market median for her experience level.

annotations.meaningVi: môi trường bị hạn chế về nguồn lực
annotations.exampleEn: Even in a constrained environment, retaining top talent should be a priority.

annotations.meaningVi: xứng đáng, có căn cứ, được bảo đảm
annotations.exampleEn: Given her performance, a promotion was more than warranted.

annotations.meaningVi: có quyền quyết định về, có thẩm quyền tự quyết
annotations.exampleEn: Managers have discretion over small expenses but need approval for larger ones.

annotations.meaningVi: sự phê duyệt từ, chữ ký chấp thuận của
annotations.exampleEn: The project required sign-off from the board before it could proceed.

annotations.meaningVi: ngưỡng hiệu suất — mức tối thiểu cần đạt để được xét đề bạt hoặc tăng lương
annotations.exampleEn: Employees who meet the performance threshold are automatically eligible for a raise.

annotations.meaningVi: mở rộng phạm vi công việc, đảm nhận thêm trách nhiệm
annotations.exampleEn: Her role expansion over two years made her case for promotion undeniable.

annotations.meaningVi: điều chỉnh lương — thay đổi mức lương cơ bản
annotations.exampleEn: A salary adjustment was overdue given the rising cost of living.

annotations.meaningVi: mô tả công việc, bản mô tả trách nhiệm của vị trí
annotations.exampleEn: Her actual duties had long since outgrown her formal job description.

annotations.meaningVi: quá trình tiếp nhận khách hàng mới
annotations.exampleEn: She streamlined the client onboarding process, reducing setup time by forty percent.

annotations.meaningVi: triển khai hệ thống quản lý quan hệ khách hàng
annotations.exampleEn: The CRM implementation was complex, but she managed it without external consultants.

annotations.meaningVi: chính thức hóa, được công nhận chính thức
annotations.exampleEn: Her informal leadership role was eventually formalised with a new title.

annotations.meaningVi: một góc độ thuyết phục, một cách tiếp cận mạnh mẽ để lập luận
annotations.exampleEn: Framing the raise as a retention issue was a strong angle in the negotiation.

annotations.meaningVi: đặt bạn vào tình thế đó, gây ra tình huống bất lợi cho bạn
annotations.exampleEn: I wouldn't put you in that position — you'll get both the title and the pay.

annotations.meaningVi: đang đi theo hướng, đang tiến triển theo chiều hướng
annotations.exampleEn: The negotiation was going in a direction that both parties felt comfortable with.

grammarNotes.title: Conditional type 2 for polite negotiation
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong đàm phán, Conditional Type 2 (If + quá khứ đơn, would + nguyên thể) được dùng để đưa ra đề xuất lịch sự mà không áp đặt. Ví dụ: 'if we were to go through the director, what would strengthen the case?' và 'if we could frame it as a role expansion…, that's an easier conversation'. Cấu trúc này làm cho yêu cầu nghe giả định hơn, ít trực tiếp hơn, do đó lịch sự hơn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In negotiation, Conditional Type 2 creates polite, hypothetical proposals without the directness of a demand. Examples: 'if we were to go through the director, what would strengthen the case?'; 'if we could frame it as a role expansion, that's an easier conversation.' The hypothetical framing reduces the pressure on the listener to respond with an immediate commitment.

grammarNotes.title: Hedging phrases for professional restraint
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong giao tiếp chuyên nghiệp cấp B2+, người nói dùng nhiều cụm từ để làm mềm khẳng định: 'I think', 'I was thinking in the region of', 'I'd want to make sure', 'I appreciate the transparency'. Những cụm này không làm yếu lập trường; chúng thể hiện sự tự tin mà không hung hăng — một kỹ năng quan trọng trong đàm phán.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Professional discourse at B2+ level uses hedging phrases to soften assertions while maintaining a clear position: 'I was thinking in the region of', 'I'd want to make sure', 'I appreciate the transparency'. These phrases signal cooperative intent without weakness, and are essential tools in high-stakes professional conversations.

translationVi:
Manager: Cảm ơn bạn đã vào, Sarah. Như bạn biết, chúng tôi đang thực hiện đánh giá hàng năm tuần này, và tôi muốn xem xét hiệu suất của bạn trong năm qua trước khi chúng ta đến phần thù lao.

Employee: Tôi trân trọng điều đó. Tôi thực sự đã thích năm vừa qua và tôi nghĩ có nhiều công việc tốt để nhìn lại. Nhưng tôi thừa nhận, cuộc trò chuyện về thù lao là cái tôi chuẩn bị kỹ nhất.

Manager: Cũng phải thôi. Để tôi bắt đầu bằng cách nói rằng hiệu suất của bạn rất xuất sắc — chỉ riêng việc cứu vãn tài khoản Henderson đã là một đóng góp đáng kể. Nhóm đã chú ý đến vai trò lãnh đạo của bạn trong dự án đó.

Employee: Cảm ơn. Dự án đó thực sự đầy thách thức. Tôi đã quản lý ba nhân viên cấp dưới, phối hợp với các tư vấn bên ngoài, và chúng tôi đã đưa khách hàng trở lại từ bờ vực hủy hợp đồng. Tôi nghĩ điều đó xứng đáng được phản ánh trong gói lương của tôi.

Manager: Về nguyên tắc tôi hoàn toàn đồng ý. Thách thức, như tôi chắc bạn biết, là chúng tôi đang hoạt động trong môi trường ngân sách thắt chặt hơn năm ngoái. Chi phí nhân sự đã tăng đáng kể trên toàn doanh nghiệp.

Employee: Tôi hiểu áp lực ngân sách, và tôi không muốn thiếu hợp lý. Nhưng tôi cũng đã nghiên cứu về mức lương thị trường cho vị trí của tôi ở cấp độ này, và hiện tại tôi đang thấp hơn mức trung vị cho các vị trí tương đương trong lĩnh vực này.

Manager: Bạn đang làm việc với con số nào, nếu bạn không ngại tôi hỏi?

Employee: Dữ liệu tôi thấy cho thấy mức trung vị cao hơn khoảng mười lăm phần trăm so với mức lương cơ bản hiện tại của tôi. Tôi không yêu cầu mười lăm phần trăm — tôi nhận ra chúng ta đang trong môi trường bị hạn chế. Nhưng tôi nghĩ một mức tăng có ý nghĩa là xứng đáng.

Manager: Tôi đánh giá cao việc bạn đến với dữ liệu thay vì chỉ một con số. 'Có ý nghĩa' trông như thế nào đối với bạn?

Employee: Tôi đang nghĩ đến khoảng mười phần trăm. Điều đó vẫn sẽ để tôi dưới mức trung vị thị trường, nhưng nó sẽ ghi nhận những đóng góp tôi đã thực hiện và thu hẹp một phần khoảng cách.

Manager: Mười phần trăm là ở phía cao của những gì tôi có quyền quyết định, thành thật mà nói. Những gì tôi có thể tự quyết định là khoảng sáu hoặc bảy phần trăm. Bất cứ điều gì vượt quá đó cần sự phê duyệt của giám đốc, điều này tốn thêm thời gian và không được đảm bảo.

Employee: Tôi đánh giá cao sự minh bạch. Tôi có thể hỏi — nếu chúng ta đi qua giám đốc, điều gì sẽ củng cố trường hợp? Đó hoàn toàn là câu hỏi ngân sách, hay có ngưỡng hiệu suất nào sẽ làm cho việc biện hộ dễ hơn không?

Manager: Thành thật mà nói, cả hai. Lập luận ngân sách là có thật, nhưng trường hợp thuyết phục hơn sẽ gắn liền với phạm vi — nếu chúng ta có thể đóng khung nó là mở rộng vai trò thay vì chỉ điều chỉnh lương, đó là cuộc trò chuyện dễ hơn để có với giám đốc.

Employee: Điều đó thực sự thú vị khi nghe. Tôi đã không chính thức đảm nhận một số trách nhiệm không có trong mô tả công việc ban đầu của tôi — đặc biệt là xung quanh việc tiếp nhận khách hàng và triển khai CRM mới. Những điều đó có thể được chính thức hóa không?

Manager: Đó là một góc độ mạnh mẽ. Nếu chúng tôi ghi lại những trách nhiệm đó và lập ra một mô tả công việc sửa đổi, tôi nghĩ có một trường hợp thực sự tốt cho việc thay đổi chức danh, điều này cũng sẽ hỗ trợ cuộc trò chuyện về lương cùng một lúc.

Employee: Tôi cởi mở với điều đó. Tôi muốn đảm bảo rằng thay đổi chức danh đi kèm với mức tăng lương thay vì được đề xuất thay thế cho nó — tôi đã nghe điều đó xảy ra trước đây.

Manager: Đó là mối lo ngại hợp lý và tôi muốn nói thẳng với bạn: tôi sẽ không đặt bạn vào vị trí đó. Ý định của tôi là theo đuổi cả hai cùng nhau. Hãy để tôi đưa vấn đề này lên giám đốc với một đề xuất đầy đủ. Tôi có thể trả lời bạn trước cuối tuần tới không?

Employee: Được. Và cảm ơn bạn đã cởi mở với cuộc trò chuyện này — tôi nghĩ nó đang đi theo hướng có thể phù hợp với cả hai bên.

questions.prompt: What does the employee say she has been most preparing for in the review?
options: Discussing her leadership style in the Henderson project
A request for additional team resources
A proposal for a new client strategy
questions.explanation: The employee says: 'the compensation conversation is the one I've been most preparing for.'
questions.hint: She admits having prepared specifically for one topic.

questions.prompt: What project does the manager specifically praise the employee for?
questions.explanation: The manager says: 'the Henderson account turnaround alone was a significant contribution.'
questions.hint: The manager names a specific account.

questions.prompt: What difficult outcome did the employee prevent in the Henderson project?
options: A data breach affecting client records
questions.explanation: The employee says she 'brought the client back from the brink of cancelling the contract.'
questions.hint: The phrase 'from the brink of' tells you how close it came.

questions.prompt: What reason does the manager give for the salary increase being challenging?
options: The employee's performance has not yet met the required threshold
The company is operating in a tighter budget environment this year
There is a company-wide pay freeze in effect
The director has already rejected a previous request
questions.explanation: The manager says: 'the challenge is that we're operating in a tighter budget environment this year than last.'
questions.hint: The manager acknowledges the employee's performance before explaining the constraint.

questions.prompt: How does the employee justify her request — what research has she done?
options: She has received a competing job offer at a higher salary
She has compared her salary to market rates for her role and level
She has calculated the revenue generated by the Henderson account
She has surveyed her colleagues about their pay
questions.explanation: The employee says: 'I've done some research into market rates for my role at this level, and I'm currently sitting below the median for comparable positions.'
questions.hint: She uses data about external benchmarks, not internal comparisons.

questions.prompt: How much above her current base does the market median sit, according to the employee's research?
questions.explanation: The employee states: 'The data I've seen puts the median at roughly fifteen percent above my current base.'
questions.hint: A specific percentage is mentioned when she describes her research findings.

questions.prompt: What salary increase does the employee actually request?
options: Fifteen percent to reach the market median
Ten percent, which would still be below the market median
Seven percent, which is within the manager's discretion
Twenty percent, to exceed the market median as a negotiating position
questions.explanation: The employee says: 'I was thinking in the region of ten percent. That would still leave me below the market median.'
questions.hint: The employee explicitly acknowledges her request would still leave her short of the median.

questions.prompt: What is the maximum the manager can authorise without going to the director?
questions.explanation: The manager says: 'What I can authorise unilaterally is closer to six or seven.'
questions.hint: The manager describes the limit of his own decision-making power.

questions.prompt: What does the employee ask about the director approval process?
options: How long the approval process typically takes
What would strengthen the case — whether it's purely budget or also performance
Whether she can speak to the director directly
What the director's policy is on salary increases above inflation
questions.explanation: The employee asks: 'what would strengthen the case? Is it purely a budget question, or is there a performance threshold that would make it easier to justify?'
questions.hint: The employee is trying to understand what arguments will be most persuasive.

questions.prompt: What framing does the manager suggest would make the director conversation easier?
options: Presenting it as a market correction to retain talent
Framing it as a role expansion rather than just a salary adjustment
Linking it to the revenue generated by the Henderson account
Emphasising the risk of the employee leaving for a competitor
questions.explanation: The manager says: 'if we could frame it as a role expansion rather than just a salary adjustment, that's an easier conversation to have with the director.'
questions.hint: The manager proposes changing the narrative around what the increase is for.

questions.prompt: What additional responsibilities has the employee informally taken on?
options: Managing the social media accounts and press communications
Client onboarding and the new CRM implementation
Training new sales staff and writing training materials
questions.explanation: The employee mentions 'client onboarding and the new CRM implementation' as responsibilities beyond her original job description.
questions.hint: She uses the phrase 'not in my original job description'.

questions.prompt: What does the manager suggest alongside a salary increase?
options: A bonus scheme linked to future performance
A title change supported by a revised job description
A promotion to a senior management position
questions.explanation: The manager says: 'I think there's a genuinely good case for a title change as well, which would support the salary conversation at the same time.'
questions.hint: The manager links two things that could be presented together.

questions.prompt: What concern does the employee raise about accepting a title change?
options: A new title might come with responsibilities she doesn't want
A title change might trigger a period of probation
She has heard of title changes being offered instead of salary increases
She worries the new title won't be recognised by industry recruiters
questions.explanation: The employee says: 'I'd want to make sure the title change comes with the salary increase rather than being offered instead of it — I've heard of that happening before.'
questions.hint: She references something she has heard about from other people's experiences.

questions.prompt: What does the manager promise about pursuing the title change and salary increase?
options: He will pursue the title change now and the salary increase after the director approves
He will pursue both together in a full proposal to the director
He will guarantee the salary increase regardless of the director's decision
He will offer a performance bonus as a bridge until a formal increase is approved
questions.explanation: The manager says: 'My intention would be to pursue both together. Let me take this to the director with a full proposal.'
questions.hint: He uses the word 'both' and the phrase 'full proposal'.

questions.prompt: By when does the manager say he can respond to the employee with an outcome?
options: By the end of this week
By the end of next week
After the board meeting next month
questions.explanation: The manager asks: 'Can I come back to you by the end of next week?'
questions.hint: A specific timeframe is mentioned in the manager's final turn.

questions.prompt: What negotiation technique does the manager compliment the employee for using?
options: Threatening to leave if she does not get a raise
Coming prepared with market data rather than just a number
Presenting her achievements in writing before the meeting
Bringing up the opinions of her colleagues for support
questions.explanation: The manager says: 'I appreciate you coming prepared with data rather than just a number.'
questions.hint: The manager explicitly contrasts what she did with a less effective alternative.

cloze.template: Manager: Thanks for coming in, Sarah. I wanted to talk through your performance over the past year before we get to the {{b1}} side of things. Employee: The compensation conversation is the one I've been most {{b2}} for. Manager: Your performance has been excellent — the Henderson account {{b3}} alone was a significant contribution. Employee: I was managing three junior staff, coordinating with external consultants, and we brought the client back from the {{b4}} of cancelling the contract. I think that deserves to be reflected in my {{b5}}. Manager: The challenge is that we're operating in a tighter {{b6}} environment this year than last. Employee: I've done some research into {{b7}} for my role at this level, and I'm currently sitting below the median for comparable positions. Manager: What would 'meaningful' look like to you? Employee: I was thinking in the region of ten percent. That would still leave me below the market {{b8}}, but it would acknowledge the contributions I've made. Manager: Ten percent is at the higher end of what I have {{b9}} over. What I can authorise {{b10}} is closer to six or seven. Employee: If we were to go through the director, what would strengthen the case? Is there a performance {{b11}} that would make it easier to justify? Manager: The more compelling case would be tied to scope — if we could frame it as a {{b12}} rather than just a salary adjustment, that's an easier conversation. Employee: I've been informally taking on some responsibilities that weren't in my original {{b13}} — particularly client onboarding. Could those be {{b14}}? Manager: If we document those responsibilities and put together a revised job description, I think there's a genuinely good case for a title change as well. Employee: I'd want to make sure the title change comes with the salary increase rather than being offered {{b15}} of it.

blanks.explanation: The manager says 'before we get to the compensation side of things' — the salary and pay discussion.
The employee says the compensation conversation is 'the one I've been most preparing for'.
The manager praises the 'Henderson account turnaround' — rescuing a struggling account.
The phrase is 'from the brink of cancelling' — meaning they were on the verge of cancelling.
The employee says contributions should be 'reflected in my package' — the full compensation bundle.
The manager refers to a 'tighter budget environment' — less money available for spending.
The employee uses 'market rates' — the going rate for similar roles in the market.
The employee refers to the 'market median' — the midpoint of salary distribution.
The manager says he has 'discretion over' a certain range — he can decide independently within that range.
The manager says 'what I can authorise unilaterally' — without needing anyone else's approval.
The employee asks if there is 'a performance threshold' — a minimum level to qualify for the increase.
The manager suggests framing it as 'a role expansion' rather than purely a pay increase.
The employee refers to tasks 'not in my original job description' — the formal list of duties.
She asks whether informal responsibilities could be 'formalised' — officially recognised.
The employee fears the title change being offered 'instead of' the salary increase.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The employee uses market data, documented contributions, and a collaborative framing strategy to negotiate a salary increase. To what extent does access to good negotiation skills and industry data create an unequal playing field in salary negotiations, and what could organisations do to make compensation decisions fairer and more transparent?

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: Thanks for coming in, Sarah.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: As you know, we're doing annual reviews this week, and I wanted to talk through your performance over the past year before we get to the compensation side of things.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I appreciate that.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I've genuinely enjoyed the year and I think there's been a lot of strong work to reflect on.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: But I'll admit, the compensation conversation is the one I've been most preparing for.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: Fair enough.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: Let me start by saying your performance has been excellent — the Henderson account turnaround alone was a significant contribution.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: The team has noticed your leadership in that project.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: Thank you.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: That project was genuinely challenging.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I was managing three junior staff, coordinating with external consultants, and we brought the client back from the brink of cancelling the contract.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I think that deserves to be reflected in my package.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: I completely agree with that in principle.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: The challenge, as I'm sure you're aware, is that we're operating in a tighter budget environment this year than last.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: Headcount costs have gone up significantly across the business.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I understand the budget pressure, and I don't want to be unreasonable.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: But I've also done some research into market rates for my role at this level, and I'm currently sitting below the median for comparable positions in this sector.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: What figure are you working with, if you don't mind me asking?

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: The data I've seen puts the median at roughly fifteen percent above my current base.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I'm not asking for fifteen percent — I recognise we're in a constrained environment.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: But I think a meaningful increase is warranted.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: I appreciate you coming prepared with data rather than just a number.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: What would 'meaningful' look like to you?

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I was thinking in the region of ten percent.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: That would still leave me below the market median, but it would acknowledge the contributions I've made and close some of the gap.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: Ten percent is at the higher end of what I have discretion over, honestly.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: What I can authorise unilaterally is closer to six or seven.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: Anything beyond that needs sign-off from the director, which adds time and isn't guaranteed.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I appreciate the transparency.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: Can I ask — if we were to go through the director, what would strengthen the case?

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: Is it purely a budget question, or is there a performance threshold that would make it easier to justify?

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: Honestly, it's both.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: The budget argument is real, but the more compelling case would be tied to scope — if we could frame it as a role expansion rather than just a salary adjustment, that's an easier conversation to have with the director.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: That's actually interesting to hear.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I've been informally taking on some responsibilities that weren't in my original job description — particularly around client onboarding and the new CRM implementation.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: Could those be formalised?

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: That's a strong angle.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: If we document those responsibilities and put together a revised job description, I think there's a genuinely good case for a title change as well, which would support the salary conversation at the same time.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I'm open to that.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: I'd want to make sure the title change comes with the salary increase rather than being offered instead of it — I've heard of that happening before.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: That's a fair concern and I want to be straight with you: I wouldn't put you in that position.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: My intention would be to pursue both together.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: Let me take this to the director with a full proposal.

sentences.speaker: Manager
sentences.text: Can I come back to you by the end of next week?

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: That works.

sentences.speaker: Employee
sentences.text: And thank you for being open to this conversation — I think it's going in a direction that could work well for both sides.
